serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of stir-fried mixed seafood with Thai curry sauce weighs approximately 450 g, providing around 780 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Seafood components (prawns, squid, fish fillet) and stir-fried mixed seafood analogues; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database for similar stir-fried seafood dishes
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai-style stir-fried mixed seafood exists in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo. Nutrient values from the input record were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual seafood components (prawns: FDC ID 175180; squid: FDC ID 175168; mixed seafood stir-fry analogues) and HPB Singapore Foodo data for similar stir-fried seafood dishes. The per-100g values are plausible and internally consistent and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and mineral data for this composite dish are not available in USDA SR Legacy or HPB Foodo databases as of 2026-04-17.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; rice noodles GI entry from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style stir-fried mixed seafood. GI estimated at 47 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (GI approximately 53, medium; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) modulated downward by the substantial protein content (10 g/100g from mixed seafood), dietary fibre (1.11 g/100g), fat (7.78 g/100g), and acidic tamarind and lime components which slow gastric emptying and reduce postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (47) x available carbohydrate per serving (450 g x 16.67 g/100g = 75.0 g) divided by 100 = 35.2. This is a high GL per full plate serving, reflecting the large portion size.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100g; a full 450 g plate delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and Thai curry paste, avoid adding condiments at the table, and limit portion size to no more than half a plate to keep sodium intake below 900 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (16.67 g/100g, predominantly from rice noodles) with a low estimated GI of 47; however, a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 75 g of available carbohydrate, producing a high glycemic load of 35.2.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g per serving to keep carbohydrate intake below 40 g per meal, request reduced palm sugar in preparation, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to further moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 77.78 mg/100g (approximately 350 mg per full plate) and sodium at 400 mg/100g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; saturated fat is relatively low at 1.78 g/100g and trans fat is negligible at 0.04 g/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion to half a plate to reduce dietary cholesterol and sodium intake, request preparation with minimal fish sauce and oil, and note that the lean seafood protein and potential omega-3 content from prawns and mussels offer partial cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mixed seafood provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory potential; however, high sodium from fish sauce may worsen tissue swelling, and nightshade-derived chilli may aggravate solanine sensitivity in susceptible individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the omega-3 content of mixed seafood but should request reduced fish sauce to limit sodium-driven inflammation and monitor personal tolerance to chilli and other nightshade ingredients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Mixed Seafood with Thai Curry Sauce (Thai: อาหารทะเลผัดรวมซอสแกงไทย; Chinese: 泰式炒海鲜) is a Thai-influenced stir-fried seafood dish served in Singapore hawker and casual dining settings. The dish typically features a combination of prawns, squid, mussels, and fish fillet wok-fried at high heat with Thai red or green curry paste, fish sauce, tamarind paste, palm sugar, lime juice, garlic, and chilli, producing a complex balance of sweet, sour, spicy, and umami flavours. Rice noodles, bean sprouts, and eggs are commonly incorporated, giving the dish a Pad Thai structural influence. The texture is characterised by tender, slightly charred seafood with a glossy, aromatic sauce coating the noodles. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 173 kcal, 10 g protein, 7.78 g fat (1.78 g saturated), 16.67 g carbohydrate, 3.33 g sugar, 1.11 g dietary fibre, and 400 mg sodium. The high sodium content (approximately 1800 mg per full 450 g plate) makes this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension unless prepared with reduced fish sauce. The estimated glycemic index is 47 (low), but the glycemic load per full serving is high at 35.2 due to the large portion of rice noodles, requiring portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Cholesterol is elevated at 77.78 mg/100g from the mixed seafood. The dish contains major allergens including crustaceans, molluscs, fish, eggs, peanuts, and soy.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry exists for this specific Thai-style stir-fried mixed seafood dish. Nutrient values from the input record (GrabFood-sourced, AI-generated at 0.7 confidence) were cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual seafood components and HPB Singapore Foodo data for similar stir-fried seafood dishes. Values are plausible and internally consistent but cannot be verified against a single exact approved-source entry. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, modulated for protein, fat, and acid content.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Thai-style stir-fried mixed seafood dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026-04-17. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the available approved sources (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, HPB Singapore Foodo). vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026-04-17. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.04 g/100g (trace level from cooking oil); this is a low but non-zero value consistent with refined vegetable oil used in stir-frying. The dish is listed as menu item 401D on GrabFood Singapore. Halal status is unconfirmed as the restaurant certification status is unknown. The dish name in the input ('401D Stir-fried Mixed Seafood with Thai') is a menu-style label; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Stir-fried Mixed Seafood with Thai Curry Sauce'. Glycemic load per serving (35.2) is high due to the large 450 g portion size despite the low GI of 47. Nutrient source: values cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (primary approved source) for seafood components including prawns (FDC ID 175180) and squid (FDC ID 175168), with HPB Singapore Foodo used as secondary cross-reference for similar Singapore stir-fried seafood dishes. GI source: University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle entry (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) used as analogous estimate basis.
